coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Trigonella foenum-graecum L seeds and their biodistribution in mice as an inorganic isotope label
coredata.dc:creator: Rezaei, Marzieh
Hekmat, Azadeh
Chamani, Jamshidkhan
Sadri, Kayvan
Darroudi, Majid
coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, researchers are paying significant attention to the processing of natural carbon sources in order to improve human safety and reduce environmental pollution by means of green synthesis methods. Based on this, the present study has been investigated the green synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with unique properties, by a natural carbon source called Trigonella foenum-graecum L (TF), through one- pot hydrothermal strategy. The results of different analysis methods (FTIR, FESEM, EDAX, TEM, UV–Vis, and PL) provided the necessary confirmations for the synthesized CQDs. Synthesized CQDs with spherical morphology and an average size of ∼ 5 nm have been a quantum efficiency of 18.9 % and optimal fluorescence under the excitation wavelength of 410 nm. Subsequently, the biological effects of CQDs (MTT assay) on CT26 cancer cell line was confirmed to be cytotoxic by observing an increase in the concentration of CQDs along with the IC50 value (∼826 μg/ml). Also, the two factors of colloidal stability and cytotoxicity, which depend on the concentration of CQDs, were considered to investigate the biological distribution of labeled 99mTc-CQDs that were intravenously injected into rats. Taken together, this work has been succeeded in providing data on the potential of CQDs for diagnostic and imaging fields in broader research.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, researchers are paying significant attention to the processing of natural carbon sources in order to improve human safety and reduce environmental pollution by means of green synthesis methods. Based on this, the present study has been investigated the green synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with unique properties, by a natural carbon source called Trigonella foenum-graecum L (TF), through one- pot hydrothermal strategy. The results of different analysis methods (FTIR, FESEM, EDAX, TEM, UV–Vis, and PL) provided the necessary confirmations for the synthesized CQDs. Synthesized CQDs with spherical morphology and an average size of ∼ 5 nm have been a quantum efficiency of 18.9 % and optimal fluorescence under the excitation wavelength of 410 nm. Subsequently, the biological effects of CQDs (MTT assay) on CT26 cancer cell line was confirmed to be cytotoxic by observing an increase in the concentration of CQDs along with the IC50 value (∼826 μg/ml). Also, the two factors of colloidal stability and cytotoxicity, which depend on the concentration of CQDs, were considered to investigate the biological distribution of labeled 99mTc-CQDs that were intravenously injected into rats. Taken together, this work has been succeeded in providing data on the potential of CQDs for diagnostic and imaging fields in broader research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the scientists of nanotechnology paid special attention to natural carbon sources (organic and biodegradable materials) in order to produce functional nanomaterials for the fields of medical treatment and diagnosis. The varying types of carbon nanomaterials (fullerene [1], nanodiamonds [2], carbon nanotubes [3], and CQDs [4]) with special capabilities, as well as quantum dots with strong fluorescence emission properties caused by their adjustable surfaces, were particularly highlighted among the research topics of scientists [5]. Several studies reported the colloidal stability and negligible cytotoxicity of their CQDs, which were synthesized through inexpensive and time-effective green methods (top-down & bottom-up) [6]. Taking these observations into consideration, the superiority of CQDs over other semiconductor quantum dots is firmly proved by their excellent biocompatibility and other indicator factors that were evaluated in various researches. Since the applicability of this product in diverse biological researches (chemical sensing [7], biosensing [8], biodistribution [9], photocatalysis [10], bioimaging [11], and fluorescence labeling [12]) is quiet undeniable, novel nanotechnology approaches with the implication of natural carbon sources were prioritized by researchers to promote the rate of biocompatibility and human health (treatment and early diagnosis) and reduce the side effects of synthetic chemicals. Considering that, there are many therapeutic and diagnostic challenges in the clinical field (biocompatibility, environmental challenges, toxicity and poor solubility) that are still unanswered. Semiconductor quantum dots (carbon dots) can be prepared from abundant and non-toxic materials (biomass) [19]. During the last decade, medicine and biology have introduced CQDs as greener and safer than other semiconductors. Considering the special properties of CQDs such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent solubility, strong and stable luminescence (exceptional photoluminescence (PL)), high specific surface area, adjustable band gap and ease of synthesis are of special importance for research [13]. Applications of CQDs have been reported in various fields (carbon fixation [14], gas storage [15], cell biology and, most interestingly, cancer imaging [16] and drug delivery [17]). These assessments led to the discovery of using the extracts of different parts of a plant (leaf, bark, stem, seeds, flowers, and root) [18], which were achieved through the conventional methods of green synthesis (hydrothermal [19], microwave-assisted [20], electrochemical oxidation [21], chemical oxidation [22], ultrasonic methods [23], etc.), for the production of CQDs with special properties. The hydrothermal method, as one of the methods of green synthesis (top-down and bottom-up), is a heterogeneous chemical reaction in the presence of a solvent (aqueous or non-aqueous) that takes place at a temperature higher than room temperature and a pressure of more than 1 atmosphere is performed. Due to the uniformity of the ions in the aqueous solution, the product obtained by the hydrothermal method has potential advantages (high purity, good dispersion and easy control of particle size) [24]. In addition, due to easy and cheap access to biological solvent (water), control of functional groups, optical properties of CQDs, environmental biocompatibility and speeding up the synthesis process of nanoparticles while improving the quality of functional groups on the surface of nanoparticles with minimal chemicals in creates the least possible time [25].

Today, nanomaterial tracers are of interest to scientists for clinical and toxicological applications. For example, conventional fluorescent staining includes staining using specific antibodies and chemical staining using fluorochrome-labeled chemical reagents, which specifically investigate the behavior of molecules in living cells. On the other hand, some fluorescence staining methods require fixation methods that are not suitable for living cells [26]. Therefore, radiotracer technology is used to perform a precise and reliable investigation in quantifying the distribution of nanoparticles in complex samples for nanomaterials toxicology research as well as clinical applications. Despite some inherent disadvantages of radiotracer techniques, which include the availability of Radionuclide/equipment, and time-consuming, health-safety considerations (radiation protection). Radiotracer technology is a powerful tool for biological and environmental tracking of nanomaterials because it has the advantages of high sensitivity [27], high penetration depth [28], and high accuracy and reliability [29], which can be combined with some spatially resolved technologies (for non-invasive and real-time detection). Therefore, to quantify the biodistribution of synthesized CQDs for focused clinical applications, technetium radioisotope has been chosen. A variety of diagnostic radiotherapy and radiation therapy applications were created in accordance with the type of emitted radiation. Intra-tissue focused radiation by radionuclides and gamma and X-ray radiation in the field of early diagnosis are considered as important factors in nuclear medicine [30]. One of the prominent radiopharmaceuticals in nuclear medicine departments is 99mTc radioisotope with a half-life of 6 h [31], which is a common radiopharmaceutical in labeling and biodistribution studies (molecules) due to its inexpensiveness and suitable properties (physical and chemical). These data signify the importance of imaging in diagnosing the early stages of a disease and observing its progress. The exertion of smart nanomaterials with fluorescence capability and high penetration coefficient in internal tissues with specific tissue targets can provide a leap in modern medicine through the aid of biological nanotechnology. Under this objective, the labeling of CQDs by radioactive tracers (radioisotopes) can purposefully determine the bioaccumulation placement of synthesized CQDs. In recent years, many studies were conducted on the labeling of CQDs and nanomaterials by the usage of different radiopharmaceuticals (99mTc [32], 123I [33], and 177Lu [34]).

Fenugreek with the scientific name Trigonella foenum graecum (L.) as an aromatic leguminous plant has a wide geographic distribution (Middle East, Asia and Europe). Therefore, it is not only easily available, but also the elements source of phosphorus, iron and sulfur. In addition, the pharmacological effects of fenugreek are due to a variety of plant compounds, including steroids, alkaloids, saponins, polyphenols, flavonoids, lipids, carbohydrates, amino acids, hydrocarbons, and a large quantity of folic acid (84 mg/100 g) [35]. It is used in traditional Indian medicine for various diseases (an aphrodisiac for rejuvenation, bronchial and digestive disorders, arthritis and gout) [36]. Also, in recent research, fenugreek (especially from the seed and leaf parts) has been used for various medicinal preparations, treatment of liver cancer, low blood pressure and diabetes [37]. Despite the medicinal properties of fenugreek, research on nanoscale bioaccumulation with focused therapeutic and diagnostic purposes has not yet been addressed. In this work, the presented extract of TF seeds in Fig. 1 was used for the preparation of CQDs through an environmentally friendly hydrothermal method under the goal of therapeutic-diagnostic applications (bio-imaging capability). In this regard, the synthesized product was subjected to certain characterization techniques such as FTIR, FESEM, EDAX, TEM, UV–Vis (Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy), and PL (Photoluminescence Spectroscopy) to determine the functional groups and check the structural and morphological changes, colloidal stability, and size in the range of nanoscale. The requested confirmations of CQDs cell toxicity and characterization analyses allowed this research to proceed into the in vivo biodistribution studies of 99mTc-CQDs in rats.

Next to the exertion of TF seeds (originated from the northern mountains of Razavi Khorasan province, Iran), other materials such as DDW (during the preparation process), mouse colorectal cancer cell line (CT26), 10 % FBS, streptomycin and penicillin (100 mg/ml), and MTT (Sigma-Aldrich, 98 %, Germany) were purchased from Merck, Germany, and grown in the RPMA culture medium. The following section implicated the investigation of biodistribution in the labeling and animal study by the usage of prepared materials including HCl (hydrochloric acid), SnCl2·2H2O (tin chloride), acetone 99 % (reducing agent), methanol 98 %, ketamine 10 %, and xylazine 2 % (Merck company, Germany). Furthermore, 99mTc was made ready in saline (Na99mTcO4) through a generator (molybdenum/technetium) of Kimia Broadcasting Company, while ROS (reactive oxygen species) was determined by the purchased DCFDA from Sigma Aldrich.

We utilized the hydrothermal method as an eco-friendly approach to synthesize CQDs. Firstly, we dried purified TF beads (1.0 g) at room temperature and then ground them. Next, the ground seeds were added to 100 ml of double distilled water. We stirred the resulting suspension under specific conditions (60 °C, 2 h). The extract obtained was then placed in a Teflon-coated stainless steel autoclave, and subjected to high temperature (180 °C, 4 h). After that, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature, and the resulting solution (dark brown) was centrifuged (15 min at 12,000 rpm) and filtered (0.22 μm). The colloidal solution was then purified further using dialysis (MWCO: 8000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the colloidal solution was stored at 4 °C for necessary tests. Fig. 2 illustrates the synthesis conditions of CQDs prepared by TF beads [38].

The powder prepared from the synthesized CQDs solution was prepared for FTIR analysis (50 mg weight). FTIR spectrum was acquired from the study of Avatar 370 infrared spectrophotometer in the spectral range (400–4000 cm−1), which exhibited a clear data on the distribution and type of functional groups throughout the synthesized CQDs.

The powder prepared from the synthesized CQDs solution was subjected to XRD study (50 mg weight). The crystallographic structure of CQDs (based on the wavelength of X-ray with Cu Kα radiation) was investigated through the application of X'PertPro-MPD device, Panalytical, Netherlands. Next to containing the nuclei of atoms with short distances of a few angstroms, the collision of crystal network with X-ray can determine the corresponding crystallographic structure as a unique XRD spectrum. Meanwhile, the impacts of X-ray are scattered throughout the amorphous materials.

The measurement of zeta potential and dynamic light scattering by a zeta sizer (SZ-100z, Horiba, Japan) helped in evaluating the colloidal stability of synthesized CQDs. After washing the target cuvettes with distilled water, they were filled with 1 ml of CQDs solution and read by the device. To get more accurate results, the zeta potential test was repeated three times.

The surface morphology of synthesized CQDs was examined by the FESEMZ DATA of a TESCAN MIRA3 device (Czech Republic). In addition, we assessed the EDX (Energy Dispersive X-rays Spectroscopy) to determine the percentage of existing elements in the obtained CQDs (at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV for 60 min). In this method, the sample (weight of 50 mg) is completely dry and subjected to the preparation process of gold-coated CQDs.

TEM images are obtained through a single particle-based approach (a gold standard technique), using electrons instead of photons. This method examines only the metal core in the dimensions of the viewing plane, and by creating two-dimensional images, it provides information about the crystal structure, size and morphology of the nanoparticles. The powder prepared from the synthesized CQDs solution in a volume of 2 Âµl (concentration 2000 mg/ml) was prepared by ultrasonic bath. Then, it was dried on a carbon plate and examined under a TEM microscope. The morphological details and size of prepared CQDs were distinguished by analyzing the TEM (Transmission Electron Microscope) data through a Zeiss device, model Leo912 AB, Germany. The sample was dried under the preparation conditions (Ultrasonic for 5 min) at room temperature on copper-carbon grid in order to configure the images by the applied software (ImageJ®).

Using UV–Vis spectrophotometry (CE9500), CQD was identified at room temperature. The UV–visible spectrophotometer has cell length (10 mm), data pitch (1.0 nm), and bandwidth (1.5 nm) at a scan speed of 400 nm/min. Certain types of nanomaterials such as CQDs have fluorescence properties due to their nanoscale size, which can determine their absorption and emission wavelengths. Photoluminescence (PL spectrum) was measured using a spectrophotometer F-2500 (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a cuvette (1.0 cm quartz).

The MTT method was exerted to measure the survival of cancer cell line (CT26) for evaluating the cytotoxic effects of CQDs, while the morphology of cell line was controlled by trypsinization (from elongated to spherical). Initially, 96-well plates containing RPMA culture medium and CT26 cancer cell line/well (2 × 104) were incubated for 24 h to maintain the stability. Thereafter, the prepared cell line was treated with the synthesized CQDs solution to be incubated again under standard conditions (37 °C, CO2) for 48 h. Subsequent to adding 5 µg/ml of MTT (20 µl) to each well, the plate was incubated for 4 h and was followed by replacing the medium with 40 μl of DMSO. An ELISA reader, Biotek Instruments, USA (wavelength 570 nm) was exerted to read the absorbance. The evaluation was carried out with three replicates as the percentage of cell survival was determined as a relative percentage of mean ± SD compared to zero concentration (untreated cells), while the inhibitory concentration (50 % cell destruction) was reported as IC50. Lastly, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) of treated cells with CQDs were examined after the addition of DCFDA solution (10 mM). Fluorescence emission of excitation wavelength at 480 nm and the emission wavelength of 530 nm (using a multi-microplate reader) was obtained after the incubation (4 h).

The labeling of synthesized CQDs with the obtained technetium from 99mMo/99mTc generator provided the functional application (diagnostic and therapeutic) of TF seeds extract. For this purpose, we prepared an aqueous solution of tin chloride that contained 2.0 mg of (SnCl2) in 2.0 ml of (0.1 M HCl) to act as the reducing agent in the labeling process of CQDs. Subsequently, 500 μl of (99mTc, 5 mCi) was added to 1.0 ml of (synthesized CQDs) and 100 μl of (SnCl2 solution), which was followed by incubating (at 1400 rpm, 20 min) the microtube of stock solution (1600 ml) to produce the desired reaction. The stability of 99mTc-CQDs was investigated and determined via ITLC (Instant Thin Layer Chromatography). The labeling efficiency (percentage) of CQDs was evaluated by the results of ITLC (on glass fiber sheets impregnated with silica-gel) in the dimensions of 5 × 15 cm as the stationary phase of chromatography. Also, chromatographic solvent reservoirs (acetone) were constituted by the mobile phases of ITLC. Moreover, we performed the 99mTc-CQDs assay (colloidal stability) in the medium of human serum and PBS at certain time intervals (Up to 24 h). The fully moistened ITLC sheets were removed from the chromatographic solvent reservoirs to be dried at room temperature and divided into three equal parts. Subsequent to placing each portion into standard tubes (RIA), the amount of radioactivity was measured by the usage of Well Gamma Delshid, DL 100 device (gamma-ray counter) [39].

In this section, cell adhesion was investigated by placing a specific amount (1 × 106) of CT26 (mouse colorectal cancer cell line) in 6-well plates under standard conditions (72 h). In the following, the supernatant was removed, washed (three times with PBS), and transferred into RIA tubes, respectively. A gamma counter (cell activity and supernatant) was exerted to determine the binding percentage of 99mTc-CQDs to the target cells.

Two-month-old rats (male Wistar breed, with a weight range of 200–250 g) were selected as an animal model to investigate the biodistribution of 99mTc-CQDs. The process was beginning by the intravenous injection (tail) of a specific volume (200 μl) of 99mTc-CQDs into the three groups (3 members) at certain time intervals (1, 4, and 24 h). Once the weights of specified organs (stomach, lungs, intestines, liver, spleen, kidneys, brain, tail, thyroid, etc.) were determined, the accumulated dose of each tissue (percentage of injected dose per gram of tissue as %ID/g) and the amount of radioactivity in each isolated tissue were measured by the application of a gamma-ray counter [38].

The usage of anesthesia, xylene, and ketamine was considered to complete the imaging process of animal models (according to the rules of the ethics committee). The imaging examination of selected rats was done to investigate the biodistribution of 99mTc-CQDs by a high-resolution gamma camera device (Siemens Double, Germany). The obtained static images (posterior, anterior) were arranged at certain time intervals (0 and 8 h) after injection with a 256 × 256 matrix and the magnification factor of 2 in 10 min [39].
Three random repetitions of the tests were performed to ensure data accuracy, reported as mean ± standard deviation (SD).

FTIR peaks were evaluated to evaluate the functional groups of CQDs synthesized by aqueous extract of TF beads. The FTIR spectrum of the aqueous extract of TF seeds confirmed the successful use of the hydrothermal method to complete the synthesis. Typical peaks of the synthesized CQDs were observed in the FTIR spectrum, compared to the aqueous extract of TF seeds. According to the FTIR spectrum of CQDs prepared by TF in Fig. 3 , structural changes in the form of wavelengths shift were recorded in some areas. The spectrum of synthesized CQDs showed a broad peak at 3411 cm−1 compared to the main peak location at 3387 cm−1 (O–H, N–H stretching vibration). Apparently, reducing the size of the synthesized CQDs has increased the amount of functional groups and surface elements [46]. Also, the absorption of moisture on the surface of CQDs has caused a broad absorption peak in the FTIR spectrum (3200–3500 cm−1) [40]. In addition, the asymmetric C–H stretching (at 2927 cm−1) throughout the synthesized CQDs solution, compared to the peak at 2928 cm−1, indicated minor structural changes and displacements [41,42]. In addition to observing the signs of a spectrum at 1760 cm−1 (C = O stretching) [43], the spectrum of 1643 and 1406 cm−1 (asymmetric and symmetric stretching of COO–) as well as 1074 cm−1 (C-O stretching) were confirmed. from the presence of saponins, in the solution of synthesized CQDs [47]. The vibrations of C = C and C-O alkoxy groups can be observed in the range of 1600–1300 cm−1 [44,45]. The presence of hydrophilic functional groups –COOH, –OH and –NH2 has caused the aqueous solubility of CQD. The results of absorption spectrum (FTIR) together with the results of zeta potential can reveal the cause of uniform and long-term dispersion for CQD as a witness [46]. However, the presence of functional groups (–CH3 and –OH) on the surface of synthesized CQDs can make them suitable candidates for drug delivery and biosensors [47,48].

In this section, we investigated the crystallographic structure of synthesized CQDs by a diffractometer equipped with monochromatic Cu Kα radiation of graphite λ = 1.54 A° in the range of 2θ (from 10° to 70°). X-ray diffraction pattern of the slow transformation of the carbon source in the extract of TF seeds (by hydrothermal synthesis method) to form small particles and an amorphous structure of CQD [49,50], with a broad single peak at about 20° with interlayer distances of 4.38 and a weak peak at 51° which corresponds to the graphite (100) crystal plane and 002 plane [51,52], respectively, has showed [53]. Also, the obtained data (XRD) has showed the purity of CQDs (caused by the complete carbonation of the extract of TF grains) [54,55]. In addition, due to C–H and O–H bonding and π-π* transitions, the colloidal stability of CQDs was observed during long storage time, and was confirmed by FTIR spectrum [56]. The X-ray diffraction pattern of synthesized CQDs is exhibited in Fig. 4 .

The quality of synthesized CQD was evaluated by the employment of UV–Vis instrument (260–800 nm) as it is displayed in Fig. 5
a. The maximum absorption peak at 283 nm (π → π*) is attributable to aromatic sp2 hybridization (C═C) and according to the obtained results, the synthesized product contained a band gap energy of 3.9 eV (calculated by Eq.
(1) . Therefore, the outcomes of UV–Vis spectrophotometer were consistent with the results of XRD pattern and FTIR spectroscopy, which emphasize the successful creation of CQDs in small sizes through the applied synthesizing process [57].

One of the attractive features of CQDs is the fluorescence caused by quantum confinement effects by size reduction [58]. According to various reports, the synthesis of CQDs can be done by diverse, natural and biodegradable carbon sources, which emits the luminescence spectrum in different wavelengths due to ultraviolet light radiation as an optical stimulus [59,60]. Different colors (including bright green, yellow or blue) can be observed by applying the appropriate excitation wavelength, which corresponds to the size and chemical functionality of the CQDs. In this regard, the optical properties of CQDs can be changed by adjusting their size, shape and geometry, as well as the nature of edges and band gaps [61]. The PL spectrum presented in Fig. 6 corresponds to CQDs synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method from the extract of TF grains without any chemical additives. To investigate the photoluminescence of CQD solution (1000 μg/ml through an ultrasonic bath) was prepared. Setting the excitation wavelength at 410 nm, the fluorescence spectrum was observed at 497 nm with the maximum scattering intensity. Also, the absorption peaks observed in the range of 380 and 410 nm can be attributed to the center of the carbon nucleus and the surface or the center of the molecule, respectively, which are known as at least two types of excitation trap energy on the surface of CQDs [59,60]. The dependence of the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs on the excitation wavelength can be seen with an increase of 10 nm (from 460 to 380 nm) through a shift to higher wavelengths (from 513 to 483 nm). Also, by optimizing the excitation wavelength through other factors (the size and amount of functional groups on the surface), the emission wavelength can be influenced [62,63].

In addition, the small size of CQD has increased the energy content of nanoparticles, which has known to be an important factor in reducing the fluorescence intensity, reducing the band gap (electron layers), and moving towards higher wavelengths (red shift). Therefore, the decrease in fluorescence intensity from the excitation wavelength of 420 nm may be influenced by several factors (CQD size, stacking, conjugated ring structure, or sp3 sites) [34,41]. According to reports, the excitability of CQDs has been possible in the range of wavelengths > 600 nm (using visible light). However, the emission of fluorescence spectra has been observed in < 600 nm regions [64]. One of the ambiguities in the evaluation of fluorescence spectra is the dependence of emission spectra on excitation wavelengths, which scientists refer to as the phenomenon of luminescence. Therefore, they use the phenomenon of luminescence in the field of imaging (diagnosis and treatment) [65]. The emission efficiency of the green light spectrum of CQDs was evaluated under the name of quantum yield (QY) by comparing with the standard reference (quinine sulfate, 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4) [66]. QY calculation through the experimental data of CQD fluorescence synthesized according to Eq.
(2) was obtained as 18.9 % [67,68].

The fluorescence QYs were displayed at a fixed excitation wavelength (360 nm) with uniform emission intensity of synthesized CQDs and standard samples (ICQDs and Is), respectively. Absorption in prepared sample and standard sample (ACQDs and As) is shown. Refractive index (η), solvents used previously. The synthesis of water-soluble carbon quantum dots is an attractive approach in modern technology. These are natural fluorescent nanomaterials with sizes < 10 nm. Researchers have widely studied the tunable luminescence of carbon quantum dots through various approaches such as synthesis and purification. For example, in 2018, Meigin He et.al were able to improve the fluorescence quantum yield for CQDs by 16.7 % by hydrothermally synthesizing CQDs from lemon juice precursor [60]. In 2020, Zubair MSH Khan et, al succeeded in synthesizing nitrogen and sulfur-doped fluorescence carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs), which showed a remarkable quantum efficiency of 53.19 % [69]. Similarly, in 2019, Yanting Xie reported a quantum efficiency of 97.32 % in the synthesis of N, Si-CQDs [70]. These reports indicate that the selected synthesis method for the preparation of CQDs has been approved and ideal. Moreover, the obtained quantum efficiency is favorable and can be further improved. Subsequently, the evaluation of the fluorescence intensity stability of pure CQDs synthesized from TF seeds extract is shown in Fig. 6 . The solution stability of CQDs evaluated over a period of 30 days showed a decrease in fluorescence intensity [71]. Nevertheless, the fluorescence intensity stability of the synthesized CQDs was observed to be ∼ 80 % after 30 days. Based on the obtained results, the synthesized CQDs can be considered as suitable candidates for evaluating biosensors and fluorescence probes [72].

The colloidal evaluation of synthesized CQDs required the application of zeta potential method (a dispersive system for indicating the electrostatic degree of charged particles that are adjacent to each other) [38]. The results confirmed the presence of repulsive forces between the particles and estimated the zeta potential value of −0.4 mV for the prepared CQDs. According to, the negative value of zeta potential indicates that electrostatic forces have created repulsion between molecules. The larger the negative charge, the greater the repulsive force between molecules. Therefore, the more CQDs dispersed systems, the more stable and the higher the zeta potential. Regarding, the synthesis of CQDs in a polar environment (water), the presence of electrostatic repulsion forces between water molecules and CQDs is observed through changes in the zeta potential distribution profile depending on the dispersion concentration of CQDs. For example, in the stock solution of synthesized CQDs or dilution of 1.2 mg/ml (concentration of 0.5 % by weight), minimum stability was observed due to initial attraction forces. Therefore, dispersed systems will be more stable than pure systems. On the other hand, for a concentration of 0.5 % by weight of CQDs, a cluster of CQDs is formed that the dipole interaction is dominant, and were led to a decrease in charge transfer (conductivity) between CQDs [73]. Also, the average size of hydrodynamic diameter (DLS) in CQDs solution was determined to be 224 nm, while the storage of this solution in a certain period of time (6 months) lacked any signs of particles accumulation [74]. The pictures of DLS analysis and zeta potential are presented in Fig. 7
a and 7b, respectively.

The size of nanoparticles can be configured through various measurement (quantitative) methods. The measurement of materials at nano scale can be done based on single particles by the approach of TEM analysis (to examine the metal core of dimensions in the viewing plane by the usage of electrons). Since the results were reported in the form of 2D images (including crystal structure, morphology, and size of nanoparticles), Fig. 8 displays the performed evaluation by TEM microscope for configuring the size and morphology of synthesized CQDs. The gathered data were indicative of a spherical morphology and the average size (using histogram) of ∼ 5 nm, which are consistent with the results of aforementioned analytical methods [75,76].

In agreement with the outcomes of TEM analysis, the FESEM images of synthesized CQDs also confirmed their spherical morphology. Furthermore, their elemental analysis indicated the weight percentage of 49.5 for carbon (C) as the first rank of existing elements, while oxygen and nitrogen were placed in the second and third ranks, respectively. The FESEM images and EDS analysis are presented in Fig. 9
.

Cytotoxicity evaluation is considered as one of the prominent sections of in vitro researches in medical applications. For this purpose, an MTT test was performed on CT26 cancer cell line in the concentration range of 16–1000 μg/ml. Fig. 10 displays the inhibition of cancer cells as a function of CQDs concentration. Cell survival at the maximum concentration of 1000 μg/ml was evidently the lowest due to observing the inhibitory concentration of 876 μg/ml (IC50) as a sign of decrease in the rate of survival [77]. The outcomes generally indicated the successful application of an organic source (with biocompatible and degradable characteristics) throughout the applied synthesizing method for CQDs. Subsequently, the treated CT26 cells with CQDs were examined by DCFDA fluorescence emission (wavelength 530 nm) to evaluate the amount of reactive oxygen species. According to Fig. 10, a significant difference was observed in the comparison of control group and the groups treated with CQDs. The observed decrease in fluorescence emission was attributable to the neutralized mechanism of metabolism through the production of reactive oxygen species in the presence of different concentrations of CQDs. The highest inhibitory effect of ROS was determined by the highest production of reactive oxygen species in the inhibitory concentration (876 μg/ml) at the rate of 1.12 %.

The stability of radiographic frequency was required to be confirmed by labeling with technetium radiopharmaceutical in order to complete the in vivo study of CQDs biodistribution. In this regard, 99mTc-CQDs were studied in human serum and PBS through instant thin layer chromatography (ITLC) at different time intervals (0, 1, 4, and 24 h). The results of the investigation into the colloidal stability of 99mTc-CQDs in acetone's mobile phase via ITLC chromatography are shown in Table 1
. The investigation of 99mTc-CQDs labeling implicated the usage of silica gel paper and acetone for the stationary and mobile phases of ITLC, respectively. The stability efficiency was determined in human serum and PBS media at the similar conditions of 0, 1, and 4 h. Meanwhile, the radiographic frequency stability of 99mTc-CQDs in human serum medium and PBS medium was 99 % and 63 % after 24 h of labeling, respectively. The outcomes of this trial affirmed the more favorable level of stability in human serum. On the other hand, the results of zeta potential assessment pointed out the occurrence of changes in the surface charge subsequent to 99mTc-CQDs labeling, which can confirm the successful attachment of 99mTc radiopharmaceutical to the surface of CQDs.

The supplementary studies of 99mTc-CQDs were conducted to evaluate the percentage of cell attachment (CT26) during the two specific concentrations of the research. A gamma counter was exerted to measure the radioactivity by transferring 99mTc-CQDs treated cells into RIA tubes after 4 h of incubation [78]. In conformity to observations, the binding percentage in high concentrations (90 μg/ml) was lower than the other volumes (30 μg/ml) due to the induced aggregation, type of morphology, and size of CQDs. The lowest and highest concentrations of 99mTc-CQDs were obtained by evaluating the percentage of connection to be 60 % and 15 %, respectively. The decrease in the percentage of cell attachment at the highest concentration was attributed to the induced increase in the permeability coefficient of CT26 (intracellular absorption). The steps of examining the cell attachment of 99mTc-CQDs are schematically drawn in Fig. 11 .

In order to design a targeted treatment strategy, it is necessary to determine the biological distribution of medicinal agents, such as nanoparticles, in the course of performing in vivo studies. Therefore, the biodistribution of CQDs is considered as an important topic of treatment and targeted diagnostics discussions in modern medicine. Taking these facts into account, the selected rats were subjected to biodistribution evaluation after the intravenous injection of 99mTc-CQDs into their tails, which were then sacrificed at certain time points (1, 4, and 24 h) to have their organs transferred separately into gamma counter tubes [38]. Table 2 presents the results of gamma counter regarding the radioactive absorption of organs in terms of %ID/g. According to the biological distribution of 99mTc-CQDs in rats at 1 and 4 h after injection with three repetitions, the bioaccumulation rates of 99mTc-CQDs in kidney tissues were 27.64 ± 1.579 and 38.74 ± 6.480, respectively. Also, certain organs such as liver, stomach, and intestine showed a better %ID/g than the other cases. Despite, the slight decrease in the bioaccumulation of 99mTc-CQDs with a value of 21.82 ± 4.850 24 h after the injection, a mild increase was observed in the amount of % ID/g for liver and spleen organs. Interestingly, 99mTc-CQDs were able to be reabsorbed by the kidney system and delivered to other organs through the blood supply system due to its small size. The tracking results of this product within 24 h proved its satisfying permeability and durability in interaction with kidney tissue cells.

Bioaccumulation was measured by planar imaging at two specific time intervals (0 and 8 h) subsequent to the intravenous injection of 99mTc-CQDs into the randomly selected rats. In order to conduct a fine imaging process, the selected rats were initially subjected to a short anesthesia through the exertion of ketamine and xylazine drugs, which were then put to sleep with their arms and legs restrained in an extended manner to capture the images by a gamma camera (with high resolution and low energy). The displayed concentration of radioactivity in the location of kidneys by these images (anterior, posterior) confirmed the results of biological distribution at the specified time points. Fig. 12 presents the scintigraphic images (anterior and posterior) obtained after the intravenous injection of 99mTc-CQDs into a rat at 0 and 8 h.

Overall, our hydrothermal green synthesis method facilitated the creation of colloidal CQDs with an average size of 5.27 nm and spherical morphology through a biocompatible method in physiological environments using TF beads. Also, emission of green fluorescence (under ultraviolet light) and quantum efficiency of 18.9 % were observed as unique features of synthesized CQDs. In this study, the level of cytotoxicity based on the concentration of synthesized CQDs to CT26 cancer cells was determined about of 876 μg/ml with the highest ROS inhibitory effect of 1.12 %. Also, the evaluation of radiographic frequency stability of 99mTc-CQDs in human serum medium was 99 % after 24 h, which has a more favorable level of colloidal stability than PBS medium. In addition, the bioaccumulation assessment of 99mTc-CQDs in rats resulted in exhibiting the amounts of 21.82 ± 4.850 % ID/g for kidney tissue after 24 h as an evaluation index. Therefore, the synthesized CQDs can take considerable parts in the supplementary stages of researches as a fluorescent tracer or in the targeting of drug delivery throughout the kidney tissue.
